| "Would you **to** consider...?" | "Would you consider...?" | Same: sin "to" |
| "We would can offer..." | "We could offer..." / "We would be able to offer..." | No se combinan dos modales |
| "I would to suggest..." | "I would suggest..." / "I'd like to suggest..." | Sin "to" después de "would" |

### 3.3 Errores con "provided that" / "as long as"

| Error ❌ | Correcto ✅ |
|---------|-----------|
| "Provided **of** the rates stay..." | "Provided **that** the rates stay..." |
| "So long **that** you agree..." | "**As long as** you agree..." / "**So long as** you agree..." |
| "On condition **to** you lower..." | "On condition **that** you lower..." |

---

## 4. Falsos Amigos en Negociaciones

| Español | Error ❌ | Correcto ✅ | "False friend" = |
|---------|---------|-----------|----------------|
| Compromiso | "Let's find a **compromise**" (queriendo decir "compromiso/obligación") | "Let's reach a **commitment**" | "Compromise" = acuerdo con cesiones mutuas (significado diferente) |
| Negocio | "This is a good **negotiation**" (queriendo decir "buen negocio") | "This is a good **deal**" | "Negotiation" = el proceso de negociar |
| Pretender | "We **pretend** to achieve savings" | "We **intend/aim** to achieve savings" | "Pretend" = fingir |
| Realizar | "We need to **realize** an agreement" | "We need to **reach** an agreement" | "Realize" = darse cuenta |
| Argumentar | "Let me **argument** my position" | "Let me **argue/present** my case" | "Argument" es sustantivo, no verbo |
| Actual | "The **actual** rates are 5%" (queriendo decir "actuales") | "The **current** rates are 5%" | "Actual" = real/verdadero |
| Eventualmente | "We will **eventually** agree" (queriendo decir "posiblemente") | "We **may/might** eventually agree" | "Eventually" = finalmente (con certeza) |
| Soportar | "We cannot **support** these conditions" (queriendo decir "soportar/aguantar") | "We cannot **tolerate/accept** these conditions" | "Support" = apoyar |

---

## 5. Errores de Registro y Tono

### 5.1 Mezclar registro formal e informal

| Error ❌ (registro inconsistente) | Problema | Correcto ✅ |
|----------------------------------|----------|-----------|
| "Dear Mr. Williams, so **basically** we need a **better deal**, **yeah**?" | Informal dentro de formato formal | "Dear Mr. Williams, we would like to discuss more competitive terms." |
| "With reference to our **chat**, I'd **appreciate it if** you could **sort out** the pricing." | Mezcla formal ("I'd appreciate") + informal ("chat", "sort out") | "Following our conversation, I'd appreciate it if you could review the pricing." |
| "We'd like to propose... **look**, the thing is..." | Empieza formal, cae en informal | "We'd like to propose... The key consideration here is..." |

### 5.2 Usar muletillas españolas traducidas

| Muletilla ❌ | Problema | Alternativa ✅ |
|-------------|----------|--------------|
| "Look, the thing is..." | Demasiado coloquial | "The key point here is..." |
| "Well, basically..." | Suena vago | (Ir directamente al punto) |
| "The truth is that..." | Implica que antes no decías la verdad | "The fact is that..." / "In reality,..." |
| "At the end of the day..." | Cliché, informal | "Ultimately..." / "When all is said and done,..." |
| "It is what it is." | Evasivo, no aporta | "We need to work within these constraints." |

---

## 6. Errores de Pronunciación en Vocabulario de Negociación

(Aplicando las reglas de word stress de Clase 20)

| Palabra | Error ❌ | Correcto ✅ |
|---------|---------|-----------|
| negotiate | ne-GO-tiate | ne-**GO**-tiate ✅ (pero el sustantivo: nego-**TIA**-tion) |
| proposal | PRO-po-sal | pro-**PO**-sal |
| agreement | a-GREE-ment ✅ | a-**GREE**-ment ✅ |
| competitive | compe-TI-tive | com-**PE**-ti-tive |
| alternative | al-ter-NA-tive | al-**TER**-na-tive |
| guarantee | GA-ran-tee | gua-ran-**TEE** (acento en -ee) |
| commitment | COM-mit-ment | com-**MIT**-ment |
| compromise | com-pro-MISE | **COM**-pro-mise |
| beneficial | be-ne-FI-cial | bene-**FI**-cial |

---

## 7. Errores en la Dinámica de la Negociación

### 7.1 No reconocer la posición del otro antes de rechazar

| Error ❌ | Correcto ✅ |
|---------|-----------|
| "No, 8% is too much." | "I understand the cost pressures you're facing, **but** 8% is above what we can accommodate." |
| "We won't pay that." | "I appreciate you sharing the rationale. **However**, we'd need to see a figure closer to 4%." |

### 7.2 No usar "we" colaborativo

| Error ❌ (confrontativo) | Correcto ✅ (colaborativo) |
|-------------------------|--------------------------|
| "**You** need to lower **your** prices." | "How can **we** find a solution that works for **both of us**?" |
| "**Your** proposal is unacceptable." | "**We** need to find a number that **we** can both work with." |
| "**I** need you to..." | "**Let's** explore how **we** can..." |

### 7.3 Cerrar sin resumir

| Error ❌ | Correcto ✅ |
|---------|-----------|
| "OK, deal. Bye." | "Let me just summarize what we've agreed on: a 4% cap with a 3-year extension and 30-day payment terms. I'll send the revised contract by Friday." |

---

## Resumen — Top 5 Errores a Eliminar

1. 🔴 **Demasiado directo**: "Lower the price" → "Would you consider reviewing the pricing?"
2. 🔴 **Sin hedging**: "That's wrong" → "I'm not sure that quite aligns with our data."
3. 🟡 **Condicionales mal formados**: "If you would lower..." → "If you lowered..."
4. 🟡 **Falsos amigos**: "compromise" ≠ compromiso, "actual" ≠ actual, "eventually" ≠ eventualmente
5. 🟡 **No reconocer antes de rechazar**: siempre "I understand... but/however..."

vocabulary.word: to be in a position to

lessons.errors_md:
# Errores Recurrentes — Finance & Budgets

> Clase 22 | Score: 4/10 — Áreas débiles: variance, OPEX, net margin, budget allocation, bottom line

---

## 1. CONFUSIÓN ENTRE TÉRMINOS SIMILARES

### Revenue vs Income vs Profit
Uno de los errores más frecuentes de hispanohablantes es usar estos tres términos como sinónimos. En español todo es "ingresos" o "beneficios", pero en inglés financiero cada uno tiene un significado preciso.

| Término | Significado real | Equivalente español |
|---------|-----------------|-------------------|
| **Revenue** | Todo lo que factura la empresa (top line) | Ingresos totales / Facturación |
| **Income** | Dinero que queda tras restar ciertos costes (más genérico) | Renta / Ingreso neto |
| **Profit** | Lo que sobra tras restar TODOS los costes | Beneficio |

- ❌ *"Our profit was €50M"* (queriendo decir facturación)
- ✅ *"Our revenue was €50M, with a net profit of €5.5M."*

**Truco:** Revenue = TOTAL de ventas. Profit = lo que SOBRA. Income = depende del contexto (gross income, net income, operating income).

### Cost vs Expense vs Expenditure
| Término | Matiz |
|---------|-------|
| **Cost** | Precio de algo, genérico → *"The cost of raw materials"* |
| **Expense** | Gasto que aparece en el P&L (ya incurrido) → *"Travel expenses"* |
| **Expenditure** | Gasto más formal, suele ser grande o planificado → *"Capital expenditure (CAPEX)"* |

- ❌ *"We need to reduce the expenditures of daily operations"* (demasiado formal para gastos del día a día)
- ✅ *"We need to reduce operating expenses"* / *"We need to cut costs"*

### Margin vs Mark-up
- **Margin** = beneficio como % del precio de venta → (Venta - Coste) / Venta
- **Mark-up** = beneficio como % del coste → (Venta - Coste) / Coste

Un producto que cuesta €60 y se vende a €100:
- Margin = 40% | Mark-up = 66.7%

- ❌ *"We have a 67% margin"* (cuando en realidad es el mark-up)
- ✅ *"We have a 40% margin and a 67% mark-up"*

---

## 2. ERRORES CON PREPOSICIONES FINANCIERAS

Las preposiciones en contexto financiero son una trampa constante. En español usamos "de" para casi todo; en inglés cada preposición cambia el significado.

### Increase / Decrease / Rise / Fall

| Estructura | Significado | Ejemplo |
|-----------|-------------|---------|
| increase **IN** something | qué sube | *"An increase **in** revenue"* |
| increase **OF** X% | cuánto sube | *"An increase **of** 15%"* |
| increase **BY** X% | en cuánto (la variación) | *"Revenue increased **by** 15%"* |
| increase **TO** X | hasta qué nivel | *"Revenue increased **to** €5M"* |
| increase **FROM** X **TO** Y | de dónde a dónde | *"Revenue increased **from** €4M **to** €5M"* |

- ❌ *"We had an increase of revenue"* → ✅ *"an increase **in** revenue"*
- ❌ *"Costs increased in 12%"* → ✅ *"Costs increased **by** 12%"*
- ❌ *"Sales rose by €3M"* (ambiguo) → ✅ *"Sales rose **to** €3M"* o *"Sales rose **by** €500K"*

### Otras preposiciones financieras clave

| Expresión | Correcto | Error típico |
|-----------|----------|-------------|
| allocate budget **to** / **for** | *"Allocate 12% of budget **to** logistics"* | ❌ *allocate budget **in*** |
| invest **in** | *"Invest **in** new technology"* | ❌ *invest **on*** |
| spend **on** | *"Spend €2M **on** automation"* | ❌ *spend **in*** |
| account **for** | *"Transport accounts **for** 40% of costs"* | ❌ *accounts **of/in*** |
| result **in** | *"This resulted **in** a 5% saving"* | ❌ *resulted **on/with*** |

---

## 3. FALSE FRIENDS — FALSOS AMIGOS EN FINANZAS

| Español | ❌ Error | ✅ Correcto en inglés |
|---------|---------|---------------------|
| **Actual** (= real, de ahora) | *"The actual budget is..."* (= el presupuesto de ahora) | **Current** → *"The current budget is..."* |
| | | "Actual" en inglés = **real, verdadero** → *"The actual cost was higher than estimated"* |
| **Eventual** (= posible, hipotético) | *"The eventual losses..."* | **Potential / Possible** → *"The potential losses..."* |
| | | "Eventual" en inglés = **final, definitivo** → *"The eventual outcome was positive"* |
| **Realizar** (= hacer, llevar a cabo) | *"We need to realize the budget"* | **Prepare / Draft / Execute** → *"We need to prepare the budget"* |
| | | "Realize" en inglés = **darse cuenta de** → *"I realized the error"* |
| **Rentable** | *"The project is rentable"* | **Profitable** → *"The project is profitable"* |
| **Compromiso** (= acuerdo) | *"We have a compromise on costs"* | **Agreement / Commitment** → *"We have an agreement on costs"* |
| | | "Compromise" en inglés = **punto medio entre dos posturas** |
| **Balance** (= resultado final) | *"The balance of the year was positive"* | **Result / Outcome** → *"The year-end result was positive"* |
| | | "Balance" en inglés financiero = **balance sheet** (estado contable) |

---

## 4. ERRORES CON NÚMEROS Y PORCENTAJES

### Expresar cifras grandes
- ❌ *"50 millions"* → ✅ *"50 million"* (sin -s cuando va con número)
- ❌ *"Two thousands"* → ✅ *"Two thousand"*
- Pero: *"Millions of euros were lost"* (con -s cuando es indefinido)

### Expresar porcentajes
- ❌ *"The 15% of the budget"* → ✅ *"15% of the budget"* (sin "the")
- ❌ *"A 15 per cent"* → ✅ *"15 percent"* o *"15 per cent"* (sin artículo antes del número)
- Pero: ✅ *"A 15% increase"* (aquí el artículo va con "increase", no con el porcentaje)

### Percentage points vs Percent
- *"Margin improved from 8% to 11%"* = subió **3 percentage points** (pp), NO 3%
- Un aumento del 8% al 11% es técnicamente un aumento del 37.5% (relativo)
- ❌ *"Margin improved 3%"* → ✅ *"Margin improved 3 percentage points"* o *"Margin improved by 3pp"*

### Decimales
- En español: 2.500,50 → En inglés: **2,500.50** (punto y coma INVERTIDOS)
- ❌ *"Revenue was 2.5 millions"* → ✅ *"Revenue was 2.5 million"*

---

## 5. ERRORES ESPECÍFICOS DE SERGIO (Score 4/10)

### Variance
**Error probable:** No distinguir variance (desviación) de difference (diferencia simple).

- **Variance** = término técnico financiero: desviación entre lo presupuestado y lo real
- Solo se usa en contexto de forecasting / budgeting
- *"We had a negative variance of €200K"* = gastamos €200K más de lo previsto

**Collocations obligatorias:**
- *"favorable/unfavorable variance"* — NOT ❌ *"positive/negative difference"*
- *"budget variance"* — NOT ❌ *"budget deviation"* (deviation existe pero variance es el estándar)
- *"variance analysis"* — el proceso de analizar las desviaciones
- *"to explain the variance"* — NOT ❌ *"to justify the deviation"*

### OPEX
**Error probable:** Confundir OPEX con "costs" genéricos o con CAPEX.

- OPEX = gastos operativos RECURRENTES (salarios, alquiler, fuel, mantenimiento)
- CAPEX = inversiones PUNTUALES (maquinaria, software, flota)
- ❌ *"We invested €500K in OPEX"* → ✅ *"We spent €500K on OPEX"* (OPEX no es inversión)
- ❌ *"OPEX for the new warehouse"* → ✅ *"CAPEX for the new warehouse"* (construir es CAPEX)

**Frase clave:** *"Converting CAPEX to OPEX"* = pasar de comprar a alquilar/SaaS. Muy común hoy.

### Net margin
**Error probable:** Confundir con gross margin o no saber la diferencia.

Recuerda la cascada:
1. **Gross margin** = Revenue - COGS (antes de gastos operativos)
2. **Operating margin** = después de OPEX
3. **Net margin** = después de TODO (impuestos, intereses, depreciación)

- ❌ *"Our net margin is 45%"* (probablemente es gross margin; net margin del 45% sería excepcional)
- ✅ *"Our gross margin is 45%, but net margin is around 11%"*

### Budget allocation
**Error probable:** Traducción literal o uso incorrecto de la preposición.

- ❌ *"The allocation of budget in logistics"*
- ✅ *"The budget allocation **for** logistics"* o *"Budget allocated **to** logistics"*
- ❌ *"We need to allocate more budget"* (incompleto — ¿a qué?)
- ✅ *"We need to allocate more budget **to** technology initiatives"*

### Bottom line
**Error probable:** Usar literalmente o no entender el doble sentido.

- Sentido financiero: **resultado neto** (última línea del P&L)
  - *"This will improve the bottom line"* = mejora el beneficio neto
- Sentido figurado: **lo esencial, el punto clave** (muy usado en business English)
  - *"The bottom line is we can't afford it"* = en resumidas cuentas, no nos lo podemos permitir

- ❌ *"The bottom line of the project is positive"* (confuso — ¿financiero o figurado?)
- ✅ Financiero: *"The project's net profit is positive"*
- ✅ Figurado: *"The bottom line is: this project works"*

---

## 6. CHEAT SHEET — FRASES DE RESCATE

Cuando no estés seguro de un término, estas frases te permiten explicar el concepto sin arriesgarte:

| Situación | Frase de rescate |
|-----------|-----------------|
| No recuerdas "variance" | *"The gap between forecast and actual results"* |
| No recuerdas "OPEX" | *"Our day-to-day running costs"* |
| No recuerdas "net margin" | *"What's left after all costs, taxes, and expenses"* |
| No recuerdas "budget allocation" | *"How we distribute the budget across departments"* |
| No recuerdas "bottom line" | *"The final profit figure"* / *"In summary..."* |

---

## 7. EJERCICIO RÁPIDO DE AUTOEVALUACIÓN

Corrige estas frases (respuestas abajo):

1. *"Our actual costs are 2.5 millions euros."*
2. *"Revenue increased in 15% compared to the last year."*
3. *"The project is very rentable with a ROI of 200%."*
4. *"We had a positive difference of €100K in the budget."*
5. *"The bottom line of Q3 was that margins improved."*

### Respuestas:

1. ✅ *"Our **current** costs are **2.5 million** euros."* (actual → current; millions → million)
2. ✅ *"Revenue increased **by** 15% compared to **last year**."* (in → by; the last year → last year)
3. ✅ *"The project is very **profitable** with **an** ROI of 200%."* (rentable → profitable; a ROI → an ROI)
4. ✅ *"We had a **favorable variance** of €100K **on** the budget."* (positive difference → favorable variance; in → on)
5. ✅ *"The **net result** for Q3 **showed that** margins improved."* (bottom line aquí mezcla sentidos — clarificar)

lessons.content_md:
# Cleft Sentences — Full Theory

## What are cleft sentences?

"Cleft" = split. You SPLIT a normal sentence to spotlight ONE part. Like a highlighter in speech. C1 speakers use these constantly in presentations, meetings, negotiations.

Normal: *"Transport costs ruined the budget."*
Cleft: *"It was transport costs that ruined the budget."*

---

## Structure 1: It is/was... that/who...

**Formula:** It + is/was + FOCUS + that/who + rest of sentence
Use **who** for people, **that** for everything else.

### Emphasizing WHAT:
*"It was the driver shortage that caused the delays."*
(not fuel, not weather — the driver shortage)

### Emphasizing WHO:
*"It was our logistics team who delivered the 2% cost reduction."*
(not consultants, not management — our team)

### Emphasizing WHEN:
*"It was in Q3 that we first noticed the OTIF decline."*

### Emphasizing WHERE:
*"It's in the last mile that 53% of our costs are concentrated."*

### Negative form (very powerful in debates):
*"It wasn't the technology that failed — it was the implementation."*
*"It isn't cost that worries me — it's time to market."*

---

## Structure 2: What... is/was...

**Formula:** What + subject + verb + is/was + FOCUS

Most natural-sounding cleft. Use to open presentations, redirect conversations, clarify position.

### Needs:
*"What we need is better visibility across the supply chain."*
*"What the board wants is a concrete action plan, not another PowerPoint."*

### Achievements:
*"What we achieved this quarter is a 2% cost reduction while maintaining OTIF."*

### Opinions:
*"What I believe is that automation pays off in year two, not year one."*
*"What concerns me most is the lead time from Asian suppliers."*

### Advanced variations:
*"What I'm trying to say is that we can't keep absorbing fuel surcharges."*
*"What makes this project different is the real-time tracking capability."*
*"What happened was that the carrier cancelled 48 hours before delivery."*

---

## Structure 3: The reason... is that...

**Formula:** The reason (why) + situation + is/was + that + CAUSE

*"The reason OTIF dropped is that three key carriers went on strike simultaneously."*
*"The reason we chose this vendor is that their API integrates with our WMS."*
*"The reason I'm recommending this approach is that it's been validated at DIA Group."*

⚠️ **Common mistake:** ❌ "The reason is because..."
"Reason" already means "because". Always: ✅ "The reason is **that**..."

---

## Structure 4 (Bonus): The thing/person/place...

More informal but useful in meetings:
*"The thing that worries me most is warehouse capacity."*
*"The person who really drove this project was María from operations."*
*"The place where we lose the most time is the cross-docking area."*

---

## When to use cleft sentences

| Situation | Example |
|-----------|---------|
| Presentations | "What we achieved is..." |
| Job interviews | "What attracted me to this role is..." |
| Negotiations | "It's not the price — it's the lead time." |
| Meetings | "The reason I called this meeting is that..." |
| Disagreeing politely | "What I'd suggest is a different approach." |
| Email openings | "What I wanted to highlight is..." |

---

## Interview Power Move

*"What sets me apart is that I've managed 400+ delivery points at Clarel while implementing AI-driven route optimization. It wasn't just about cutting costs — it was about building a system that scales."*

Two clefts in one answer = C1 guaranteed.
lessons.errors_md:
# Errores Recurrentes — Cleft Sentences

> Clase 23 | Score: 6/6 — Errores menores: spelling "renegociate" → negotiate, "returns" plural, "costs" 3rd person

---

## 1. ERRORES DE ORDEN DE PALABRAS

### Error #1: Poner el verbo antes del sujeto en la cláusula what
En español, el orden es más flexible. En inglés, las cleft sentences con "what" SIEMPRE siguen: What + S + V + is/was + FOCO.

- ❌ *"What concerns me is the costs are rising"* (falta estructura)
- ✅ *"What concerns me is **that** the costs are rising"*
- ❌ *"What is important is reduce costs"* (falta infinitivo con "to")
- ✅ *"What is important is **to** reduce costs"*

### Error #2: Invertir el orden del cleft
- ❌ *"It was that ruined the budget transport costs"*
- ✅ *"It was transport costs that ruined the budget"*

La estructura es FIJA: It + is/was + FOCO + that/who + resto.

### Error #3: Omitir "that" cuando es necesario
- ❌ *"It was the shortage caused the delay"*
- ✅ *"It was the shortage **that** caused the delay"*

En "It is/was..." clefts, el **that/who** es obligatorio (a diferencia de relative clauses donde a veces se omite).

---

## 2. ERRORES DE CONCORDANCIA TEMPORAL

### El verbo "be" debe concordar con el TIEMPO de la acción

| Tiempo de la acción | Cleft correcto |
|---------------------|---------------|
| Presente | *"It **is** the cost **that** worries me"* |
| Pasado | *"It **was** the cost **that** worried me"* |
| Presente perfecto | *"It **is** the team **that has** delivered results"* |

### Error frecuente: Mezclar tiempos
- ❌ *"It was the new system that **improves** efficiency"* (was + improves = inconsistente)
- ✅ *"It **is** the new system that **improves** efficiency"* (presente + presente)
- ✅ *"It **was** the new system that **improved** efficiency"* (pasado + pasado)

### Error con "What" clefts
- ❌ *"What we need was better technology"* (need = presente, was = pasado)
- ✅ *"What we need **is** better technology"*
- ✅ *"What we **needed** was better technology"*

---

## 3. SOBREUSO DE CLEFT SENTENCES

### Cuándo NO usar cleft sentences

Las cleft sentences son para **enfatizar** un elemento específico. Si no hay contraste ni necesidad de foco, una frase normal es mejor.

- ❌ *"It was yesterday that I went to the office"* (si no hay contraste, sobra)
- ✅ *"I went to the office yesterday"*
- ❌ *"What I did was eat lunch"* (trivial, sin énfasis real)
- ✅ *"I had lunch"*

### Regla de oro: Usa cleft sentences cuando hay CONTRASTE o CORRECCIÓN

| Situación | ¿Cleft? | Ejemplo |
|-----------|---------|---------|
| Corregir a alguien | ✅ SÍ | *"It wasn't the price — it was the lead time"* |
| Destacar un logro | ✅ SÍ | *"What we achieved is a 7% OTIF improvement"* |
| Dar una razón | ✅ SÍ | *"The reason we chose this vendor is that..."* |
| Contar algo normal | ❌ NO | ❌ *"It was in Madrid that I live"* → ✅ *"I live in Madrid"* |
| Descripción neutra | ❌ NO | ❌ *"What happened was that I went home"* → ✅ *"I went home"* |

### El "síndrome del C1 nuevo"
Cuando aprendes cleft sentences, tiendes a meterlas en TODAS las frases. Esto suena artificial. Usa 1-2 por respuesta larga, no más.

---

## 4. ERRORES CON "THE REASON... IS THAT"

### Error universal: "The reason is BECAUSE"
- ❌ *"The reason we failed is **because** the market crashed"*
- ✅ *"The reason we failed is **that** the market crashed"*

"Reason" ya significa "because". Decir "reason... because" es como decir "la razón es porque" — redundante.

### Error: Omitir "that"
- ❌ *"The reason is we didn't have enough stock"*
- ✅ *"The reason is **that** we didn't have enough stock"*

Aunque en inglés informal se omite, en C1 formal/escrito siempre incluye "that".

---

## 5. ERRORES DE SPELLING RELACIONADOS (Errores de Sergio)

### "Negotiate" — NO "negociate"
El español "negociar" engaña. En inglés:
- ✅ **negotiate** /nɪˈɡəʊʃieɪt/
- ❌ ~~negociate~~ (no existe)
- Familia completa: negotiate → negotiation → negotiator → negotiable

### Plurales y terceras personas en contexto cleft
Dentro de una cleft sentence, las reglas gramaticales normales siguen aplicando:

- ❌ *"It is the high return that attract investors"*
- ✅ *"It is the high return**s** that attract investors"* (returns = plural en finanzas)
- ✅ *"It is the high return that attract**s** investors"* (si return es singular, el verbo lleva -s)

**Con "costs":**
- "Cost" como sustantivo contable → plural "costs": *"It was the rising **costs** that caused the problem"*
- "Cost" como verbo en 3ª persona → "costs": *"What **costs** the most is last-mile delivery"*

---

## 6. ERRORES DE REGISTRO

### Demasiado informal para contexto profesional
- ❌ *"The thing is, we lost money"* (muy coloquial)
- ✅ *"The fact is that we incurred a loss"*
- ✅ *"What happened was that we incurred a loss"*

### Demasiado recargado para conversación
- ❌ *"It is precisely due to the fact that our operational expenditure exceeded the allocated budget that I am of the opinion that..."*
- ✅ *"It's the overspending on OPEX that concerns me"*

---

## 7. TABLA RESUMEN DE ERRORES

| Error | ❌ Incorrecto | ✅ Correcto |
|-------|-------------|-----------|
| Reason + because | *The reason is because...* | *The reason is that...* |
| Tiempos mezclados | *It was X that improves...* | *It was X that improved...* |
| Falta "that" | *It was the cost caused...* | *It was the cost that caused...* |
| Sobreuso | *It was at 9am that I started work* | *I started work at 9am* |
| Negotiate spelling | *renegociate* | *renegotiate* |
| Concordancia | *the returns that attracts* | *the returns that attract* |
| What + infinitivo | *What is important is reduce* | *What is important is to reduce* |

---

## 8. EJERCICIO RÁPIDO — DETECTA EL ERROR

Identifica y corrige el error en cada frase:

1. *"The reason we changed suppliers is because they were unreliable."*
2. *"It was the logistics team that have delivered the savings."*
3. *"What we need was a better forecasting tool."*
4. *"It was in Q3 the problems started."*
5. *"What is important is reduce our lead times by 20%."*

### Respuestas:

1. ✅ *"The reason we changed suppliers is **that** they were unreliable."* (because → that)
2. ✅ *"It was the logistics team that **delivered** the savings."* (It was → pasado, por tanto delivered sin have)
3. ✅ *"What we need **is** a better forecasting tool."* (need = presente → is, no was)
4. ✅ *"It was in Q3 **that** the problems started."* (falta "that")
5. ✅ *"What is important is **to** reduce our lead times by 20%."* (falta "to")

vocabulary.word: as far as x is concerned
vocabulary.translation: En lo que se refiere a X

lessons.errors_md:
# Errores Recurrentes — Word Stress & Pronunciation

> Clase 24 | Score: 7/10 — Errores: INfrastructure (no inFRA), neGOtiate (no NE), straTEgic (no STRA)

---

## 1. LA INTERFERENCIA DEL ESPAÑOL EN EL ACENTO PROSÓDICO

### Por qué los hispanohablantes fallan en word stress

El español tiene reglas de acentuación **predecibles y transparentes** (la tilde te dice dónde va el acento). El inglés NO tiene un sistema regular — el acento depende del origen de la palabra, su función gramatical y, muchas veces, simplemente hay que memorizarlo.

**Resultado:** Los hispanohablantes tienden a aplicar el patrón acentual español a palabras inglesas. Tres tendencias peligrosas:

### Tendencia 1: Acentuar la penúltima sílaba (patrón llano español)
El español tiene mayoría de palabras llanas (acento en penúltima). Esto lleva a errores como:

| Palabra | ❌ Patrón español | ✅ Patrón inglés |
|---------|------------------|-----------------|
| infrastructure | in-fra-STRUC-ture | **IN**-fra-struc-ture |
| comfortable | com-for-TA-ble | **COM**-for-ta-ble |
| vegetable | ve-ge-TA-ble | **VEG**-e-ta-ble |
| interesting | in-te-RES-ting | **IN**-te-res-ting |
| preferable | pre-fe-RA-ble | **PREF**-e-ra-ble |
| comparable | com-pa-RA-ble | **COM**-pa-ra-ble |

### Tendencia 2: Acentuar la primera sílaba de palabras cognadas
Muchas palabras inglesas vienen del latín/español, pero el acento se ha MOVIDO. Los hispanohablantes ponen el acento donde estaría en español:

| Palabra | Español | ❌ Error | ✅ Correcto |
|---------|---------|---------|-----------|
| negotiate | ne-GO-ciar | **NE**-go-ti-ate | ne-**GO**-ti-ate |
| strategic | es-tra-TÉ-gi-co | **STRA**-te-gic | stra-**TE**-gic |
| development | de-sa-RRO-llo | de-ve-**LOP**-ment | de-**VE**-lop-ment |
| certificate | cer-ti-fi-CA-do | cer-ti-**FI**-cate | cer-**TI**-fi-cate |
| competitive | com-pe-ti-TI-vo | com-pe-**TI**-tive | com-**PE**-ti-tive |

### Tendencia 3: Dar el mismo peso a todas las sílabas
El español es una lengua "syllable-timed" (cada sílaba dura más o menos lo mismo). El inglés es "stress-timed" (las sílabas acentuadas son largas y claras; las átonas se "tragan").

- ❌ Español style: "com-MU-ni-CA-tion" (todas las sílabas marcadas)
- ✅ English style: "com-mu-ni-**CA**-tion" (solo una sílaba destaca; el resto se reducen)

---

## 2. ERRORES ESPECÍFICOS DE SERGIO

### INfrastructure (no inFRAstructure)
- ✅ **/ˈɪnfrəstrʌktʃər/** → **IN**-fra-struc-ture
- La primera sílaba lleva el acento principal
- El error viene de asociar con "infraestructura" (in-fra-es-truc-TU-ra en español)
- **Truco:** Piensa en "INfra" como un prefijo corto y seco, como un golpe

### neGOtiate (no NEgotiate)
- ✅ **/nɪˈɡəʊʃieɪt/** → ne-**GO**-ti-ate
- Segunda sílaba acentuada
- En español "negociar" lleva el acento en la "a" (ne-go-ci-AR) — completamente diferente
- **Toda la familia:** ne**GO**tiate, nego**TIA**tion, ne**GO**tiator, ne**GO**tiable

### straTEgic (no STRAtegic)
- ✅ **/strəˈtiːdʒɪk/** → stra-**TE**-gic
- Regla -ic: el acento va en la sílaba ANTES de "-ic"
- Pero OJO: **STRA**-te-gy (la raíz tiene acento en la primera sílaba)
- **El acento se MUEVE:** STRAtegy → straTEgic → straTEgically

---

## 3. REGLAS QUE LOS HISPANOHABLANTES OLVIDAN

### Regla del sufijo -ic: Acento en la sílaba ANTERIOR
Esta regla es 100% fiable y los hispanohablantes la ignoran constantemente:

| Raíz | + -ic | Acento |
|------|-------|--------|
| STRAtegy | straTEgic | se mueve |
| ECOnomy | ecoNOMic | se mueve |
| eLECtric | eLECtric | no cambia (ya está) |
| autoMAtion | autoMATic | se mueve |
| CLImate | cliMATic | se mueve |
| SCIence | scienTIFic | se mueve |

### Regla del sufijo -tion/-sion: Acento en la sílaba ANTERIOR
Siempre, sin excepción:

| Palabra | Acento |
|---------|--------|
| nego**TIA**tion | penúltima antes de -tion |
| organi**ZA**tion | penúltima antes de -tion |
| distri**BU**tion | penúltima antes de -tion |
| optimi**ZA**tion | penúltima antes de -tion |
| de**CI**sion | penúltima antes de -sion |

### Regla noun/verb que los españoles ignoran
En español, "exportar" y "exportación" tienen acento predecible. En inglés, el MISMO par de letras cambia de acento según sea nombre o verbo:

| Nombre (1ª sílaba) | Verbo (2ª sílaba) |
|--------------------|-------------------|
| **EX**port | ex**PORT** |
| **IM**port | im**PORT** |
| **RE**cord | re**CORD** |
| **PRO**duce | pro**DUCE** |
| **CON**tract | con**TRACT** |
| **PRE**sent | pre**SENT** |
| **PRO**ject | pro**JECT** |
| **IN**crease | in**CREASE** |

- *"Spain's **EX**ports grew 5%."* (nombre)
- *"Spain ex**PORTS** olive oil worldwide."* (verbo)

---

## 4. SENTENCE STRESS — ERRORES TÍPICOS

### Error: Dar peso a TODAS las palabras
El inglés tiene un ritmo basado en acentos. Las palabras "de contenido" (sustantivos, verbos principales, adjetivos, adverbios, negaciones) se acentúan. Las "funcionales" (artículos, preposiciones, pronombres, auxiliares) se reducen.

- ❌ **THE** **COSTS** **HAVE** **INCREASED** **BY** **FIFTEEN** **PERCENT**
- ✅ the **COSTS** have in**CREASED** by fif**TEEN** per**CENT**

Las palabras "the", "have", "by" casi desaparecen en habla natural.

### Error: No reducir vocales átonas
En inglés, las vocales en sílabas no acentuadas se convierten en "schwa" /ə/ (un sonido neutro, casi mudo). Los hispanohablantes pronuncian cada vocal claramente.

| Palabra | ❌ Español style | ✅ English (con schwas) |
|---------|-----------------|----------------------|
| percentage | per-cen-TA-ge | per-**CEN**-tidge /pəˈsentɪdʒ/ |
| company | COM-pa-ny | **COM**-puh-ny /ˈkʌmpəni/ |
| management | MA-na-ge-ment | **MAN**-ij-ment /ˈmænɪdʒmənt/ |

---

## 5. PALABRAS DE NEGOCIOS MÁS PROBLEMÁTICAS

Lista de las 15 palabras que más fallan los hispanohablantes en contexto profesional:

| Palabra | ❌ Error común | ✅ Correcto | IPA |
|---------|-------------|-----------|-----|
| infrastructure | in-FRA-struc-ture | **IN**-fra-struc-ture | /ˈɪnfrəstrʌktʃər/ |
| negotiate | NE-go-ti-ate | ne-**GO**-ti-ate | /nɪˈɡəʊʃieɪt/ |
| strategic | STRA-te-gic | stra-**TE**-gic | /strəˈtiːdʒɪk/ |
| development | de-ve-LOP-ment | de-**VE**-lop-ment | /dɪˈveləpmənt/ |
| competitive | com-pe-TI-tive | com-**PE**-ti-tive | /kəmˈpetɪtɪv/ |
| sustainable | sus-tai-NA-ble | sus-**TAI**-na-ble | /səˈsteɪnəbl/ |
| certificate | cer-ti-FI-cate | cer-**TI**-fi-cate | /sərˈtɪfɪkət/ |
| comfortable | com-FOR-ta-ble | **COM**-for-ta-ble | /ˈkʌmftəbl/ |
| preferable | pre-FER-a-ble | **PREF**-er-a-ble | /ˈprefərəbl/ |
| analysis | a-NA-ly-sis | a-**NA**-ly-sis | /əˈnæləsɪs/ |
| comparable | com-PA-ra-ble | **COM**-pa-ra-ble | /ˈkɒmpərəbl/ |
| determine | de-ter-MI-ne | de-**TER**-mine | /dɪˈtɜːrmɪn/ |
| executive | e-xe-CU-tive | ex-**EC**-u-tive | /ɪɡˈzekjʊtɪv/ |
| employee | em-PLO-yee | em-ploy-**EE** | /ɪmˌplɔɪˈiː/ |
| guarantee | GA-ran-tee | gua-ran-**TEE** | /ˌɡærənˈtiː/ |

---

## 6. EJERCICIO RÁPIDO DE AUTOEVALUACIÓN

Lee estas frases en voz alta. ¿Dónde va el acento en las palabras en **negrita**?

1. *"The **strategic** **development** of our **infrastructure** is a priority."*
2. *"We need to **negotiate** a **competitive** **contract**."*
3. *"**Employee** **productivity** has **increased** significantly."*

### Respuestas:

1. stra**TE**gic / de**VE**lopment / **IN**frastructure
2. ne**GO**tiate / com**PE**titive / **CON**tract (nombre)
3. employ**EE** / produc**TI**vity / in**CREASED** (verbo)

lessons.content_md:
# 📖 LECCIÓN GRAMATICAL — El Subjuntivo en Inglés (Subjunctive Mood)
**Clase 25** | Nivel B2→C1

---

## 1. ¿Qué es el subjuntivo en inglés?

El **subjunctive mood** (modo subjuntivo) es una estructura gramatical que se usa en inglés formal y de negocios para expresar **sugerencias, demandas, recomendaciones y necesidades**. A diferencia del español donde el subjuntivo es muy común ("quiero que vayas", "es necesario que sepa"), en inglés se usa específicamente después de ciertos verbos y expresiones.

**La regla fundamental**: Después de verbos de mandato/sugerencia + "that", el verbo va en su **forma base** (infinitivo sin "to"), SIN conjugar, SIN añadir -s, SIN usar auxiliares.

> ⚠️ En español decimos "Recomiendo que **vaya**" (subjuntivo conjugado). En inglés: "I recommend that he **go**" (forma base, sin conjugar).

---

## 2. Formación

| Persona | Indicativo (normal) | Subjuntivo |
|---------|---------------------|------------|
| I | I **go** | that I **go** |
| He/She | He **goes** | that he **go** ← ¡SIN -s! |
| We | We **are** | that we **be** ← ¡Siempre BE! |
| They | They **have** | that they **have** |
| It | It **is** | that it **be** ← ¡Siempre BE! |

**Clave absoluta**: El verbo "to be" en subjuntivo es SIEMPRE "**be**", nunca "is", "are", "was" o "were".

---

## 3. ¿Cuándo se usa? — Los tres disparadores

### A) Verbos de mandato/sugerencia + "that"

Estos verbos "disparan" el subjuntivo cuando van seguidos de una cláusula con "that":

| Verbo | Significado | Ejemplo |
|-------|-------------|---------|
| **suggest** | sugerir | I suggest that he **attend** the meeting. |
| **recommend** | recomendar | She recommends that the policy **be** updated. |
| **demand** | exigir | They demand that the supplier **deliver** on time. |
| **insist** | insistir | He insists that the report **be** ready by Friday. |
| **request** | solicitar | We request that the client **provide** documentation. |
| **propose** | proponer | I propose that we **hire** a new logistics manager. |
| **require** | requerir | The contract requires that the vendor **maintain** quality. |
| **urge** | instar | The board urges that management **take** immediate action. |
| **ask** | pedir | She asked that the team **submit** their reports early. |
| **advise** | aconsejar | I advise that he **not sign** the contract yet. |
| **mandate** | ordenar | The regulation mandates that companies **comply** with safety standards. |
| **stipulate** | estipular | The agreement stipulates that payment **be** made within 30 days. |

> 📌 **Truco para recordar**: Si el verbo principal expresa "quiero que alguien haga algo" → subjuntivo.

### B) Construcciones con adjetivo + "that"

| Expresión | Ejemplo |
|-----------|---------|
| It is **essential** that... | It is essential that every driver **complete** the training. |
| It is **vital** that... | It is vital that the budget **be** approved today. |
| It is **important** that... | It is important that she **be** present at the meeting. |
| It is **necessary** that... | It is necessary that the warehouse **be** audited. |
| It is **crucial** that... | It is crucial that OTIF **not drop** below 90%. |
| It is **imperative** that... | It is imperative that we **take** action immediately. |
| It is **urgent** that... | It is urgent that the supplier **send** new samples. |
| It is **desirable** that... | It is desirable that all staff **receive** training. |

> 📌 Todos estos adjetivos expresan **necesidad o importancia**. La estructura es siempre: *It is + adjetivo + that + sujeto + forma base del verbo*.

### C) Expresiones fijas (memorizar)

Estas expresiones tienen el subjuntivo "fosilizado" — son fórmulas que se usan tal cual:

| Expresión | Significado | Ejemplo |
|-----------|-------------|---------|
| If need **be** | Si fuera necesario | If need be, we'll hire temporary staff. |
| **Be** that as it may | Sea como sea | Be that as it may, we must meet the deadline. |
| God **forbid** | Dios no lo quiera | God forbid the shipment arrive late. |
| Come what **may** | Pase lo que pase | Come what may, we'll deliver on time. |
| I wish I **were** | Ojalá fuera | I wish I were in charge of that project. |
| Far **be** it from me | Lejos de mí | Far be it from me to question your decision. |
| **Suffice** it to say | Baste decir | Suffice it to say, the results were disappointing. |

---

## 4. El subjuntivo NEGATIVO

**REGLA**: Se añade "**not**" ANTES del verbo. NUNCA se usa "don't/doesn't/didn't".

| ❌ Incorrecto | ✅ Correcto |
|---------------|------------|
| ...that he **don't go** | ...that he **not go** |
| ...that it **doesn't happen** | ...that it **not happen** |
| ...that she **didn't resign** | ...that she **not resign** |

**Ejemplos completos:**
- The manager demanded that the employee **not leave** before 6 PM.
- It is essential that the data **not be** shared with third parties.
- I suggest that we **not rush** into a decision.

> ⚠️ Este es uno de los puntos donde más fallan los hispanohablantes. En español usamos "no" antes del verbo subjuntivo ("que no vaya"), y en inglés es igual ("that he not go"), pero la tentación de usar "doesn't/don't" es enorme.

---

## 5. Subjuntivo PASIVO

Cuando necesitas la voz pasiva en subjuntivo, la estructura es: **be + participio pasado**

| Activo | Pasivo |
|--------|--------|
| that he **send** the report | that the report **be sent** |
| that she **approve** the budget | that the budget **be approved** |
| that they **update** the system | that the system **be updated** |
| that we **not delay** the project | that the project **not be delayed** |

> ⚠️ **Error frecuente**: Usar "need to be" en lugar de simplemente "be".
> - ❌ "I require that it **needs to be** done"
> - ✅ "I require that it **be** done"

---

## 6. Diferencia con el inglés británico

En inglés británico, es aceptable usar "should + infinitivo" como alternativa:
- 🇺🇸 US: "I suggest that he **attend** the meeting."
- 🇬🇧 UK: "I suggest that he **should attend** the meeting."

Ambas son correctas, pero para el C1, debes dominar la forma americana (subjuntivo puro) porque es la que aparece en los exámenes.

---

## 7. Resumen visual

```
SUBJUNTIVO EN INGLÉS = Forma base del verbo (sin conjugar)

Verbo de mandato + that + sujeto + FORMA BASE
It is + adjetivo de necesidad + that + sujeto + FORMA BASE
Negativo: ... that + sujeto + NOT + FORMA BASE
Pasivo: ... that + sujeto + BE + PARTICIPIO PASADO
```

---

## 8. Aplicación práctica: Business English

El subjuntivo es especialmente frecuente en:
- **Contratos**: "The agreement stipulates that payment **be** made within 30 days."
- **Propuestas**: "I recommend that the company **invest** in new technology."
- **Actas de reunión**: "The board resolved that the CEO **be** replaced."
- **Políticas de empresa**: "Company policy requires that all employees **undergo** background checks."
- **Emails formales**: "I propose that we **schedule** a follow-up meeting."

Dominar el subjuntivo es lo que distingue un B2 competente de un C1 real en contextos profesionales.
lessons.errors_md:
# ⚠️ ERRORES RECURRENTES — Subjuntivo en Inglés
**Clase 25** | Errores típicos de hispanohablantes B2→C1

---

## Errores detectados en Sergio (Clase 25: 8/10)

### Error principal: Subjuntivo pasivo con "need to be"
- **Ejercicios fallados**: #1 y #6
- **Patrón**: Sergio usó "need to be" donde debía usar simplemente "be"
- ❌ "The logistics director recommends that the fleet **needs to be** updated."
- ✅ "The logistics director recommends that the fleet **be** updated."
- ❌ "The board insists that the budget **needs to be** approved today."
- ✅ "The board insists that the budget **be** approved today."

**¿Por qué ocurre?** En español decimos "es necesario que **sea** actualizado" — el verbo "necesitar" se confunde con la estructura "need to be" en inglés. Pero dentro de una cláusula subjuntiva, "need" no tiene cabida. La forma base "be" ya contiene toda la información necesaria.

> 🔑 **Regla para memorizar**: Dentro de "that + subjuntivo", NUNCA uses "need to be". Siempre es solo "be + participio".

---

## Catálogo completo de errores frecuentes en hispanohablantes

### 1. Añadir -s a la tercera persona
| ❌ Error | ✅ Correcto | Causa |
|----------|------------|-------|
| "I suggest that he **goes**" | "I suggest that he **go**" | Reflejo automático de conjugar la 3ª persona |
| "She demands that it **remains**" | "She demands that it **remain**" | No reconocer el contexto subjuntivo |

**Truco**: Si hay un verbo disparador + "that", el siguiente verbo NUNCA lleva -s.

### 2. Usar "is/are/was" en lugar de "be"
| ❌ Error | ✅ Correcto | Causa |
|----------|------------|-------|
| "It's vital that she **is** informed" | "It's vital that she **be** informed" | Traducción del indicativo español |
| "I insist that the report **is** ready" | "I insist that the report **be** ready" | Confusión indicativo/subjuntivo |

**Truco**: Después de verbos de mandato + "that", el verbo "to be" SIEMPRE es "be".

### 3. Usar auxiliares negativos (don't/doesn't)
| ❌ Error | ✅ Correcto | Causa |
|----------|------------|-------|
| "...that he **doesn't** come" | "...that he **not** come" | Hábito del negativo en indicativo |
| "...that they **don't** cancel" | "...that they **not** cancel" | Transferencia del patrón normal |

**Truco**: La negación subjuntiva es elegante y simple: solo "not" antes del verbo.

### 4. Usar "need to be" en pasiva subjuntiva
| ❌ Error | ✅ Correcto | Causa |
|----------|------------|-------|
| "...that it **needs to be** done" | "...that it **be** done" | Confusión con "needs to be" del inglés cotidiano |
| "...that the policy **needs to be** updated" | "...that the policy **be** updated" | "Necesita ser" traducido literalmente |

> 🎯 **Este fue el error de Sergio en clase**. Es el más insidioso porque "needs to be" suena natural fuera del subjuntivo.

### 5. Usar condicional "would" dentro del subjuntivo
| ❌ Error | ✅ Correcto | Causa |
|----------|------------|-------|
| "I suggest that he **would** go" | "I suggest that he **go**" | Confusión con condicional/cortesía |
| "It's important that she **would** be there" | "It's important that she **be** there" | Mezcla con "I would suggest..." |

### 6. Olvidar el subjuntivo completamente
| ❌ Error | ✅ Correcto | Causa |
|----------|------------|-------|
| "I suggest him to go" | "I suggest that he go" | Calcado del español "le sugiero ir" |
| "It's important she comes" | "It's important that she come" | Ignorar la estructura subjuntiva |

---

## Plan de refuerzo para Sergio

1. **Prioridad máxima**: Practicar subjuntivo pasivo (be + participio) — punto débil demostrado
2. **Prioridad alta**: Automatizar la negación sin auxiliar (not + verbo base)
3. **Mantenimiento**: Los demás puntos los domina bien (8/10 es sólido)

---

## Autoevaluación rápida

Completa sin mirar arriba. Si fallas alguna, vuelve a la sección correspondiente.

1. "The board requires that the project ___ completed by June." → **be**
2. "I insist that he ___ ___ late to meetings." → **not be** / **not arrive**
3. "Company policy demands that all employees ___ tested." → **be**
4. "She suggests that the system ___ ___ before launch." → **be updated** (NO "needs to be updated")
5. "It is crucial that costs ___ ___ increase." → **not** (that costs **not increase**)

lessons.content_md:
# 📖 LECCIÓN GRAMATICAL — Reading Comprehension + Debate Language
**Clase 26** | Nivel B2→C1

---

## PARTE 1: Reading Comprehension — Estrategias C1

En el nivel C1, no basta con entender un texto — necesitas **analizar, sintetizar y opinar** sobre él.

### 1.1 Técnicas de lectura avanzada

#### A) Skimming (lectura rápida)
- Lee el título, primer párrafo, primera frase de cada párrafo y conclusión
- **Objetivo**: captar la idea general en 1-2 minutos
- **Pregunta clave**: "¿De qué trata?" → responder en 1 frase
- **Cuándo usarlo**: Primera aproximación a cualquier texto largo

#### B) Scanning (búsqueda específica)
- Buscar datos concretos: cifras, nombres, fechas
- **Objetivo**: responder preguntas factuales sin releer todo
- **Truco**: buscar palabras clave de la pregunta en el texto
- **Cuándo usarlo**: Preguntas tipo "What percentage...?", "When did...?"

#### C) Intensive reading (lectura profunda)
- Leer párrafo a párrafo, identificar:
  - **Argumento principal** del autor
  - **Evidencia** que lo sustenta
  - **Vocabulario nuevo** en contexto
  - **Tono**: ¿neutral, crítico, optimista?
- **Cuándo usarlo**: Preguntas de análisis y opinión

#### D) Inference (inferencia) — LA habilidad C1
- Lo que el autor **NO dice explícitamente** pero implica
- Estructuras clave para respuestas inferenciales:
  - "The author suggests that..."
  - "It can be inferred that..."
  - "The implication is that..."
  - "This passage implies..."
- **Cuándo usarlo**: Preguntas tipo "What does the author imply?", "Why does the author mention...?"

> 📌 **La inferencia es LA habilidad que marca el C1**. Un B2 entiende lo que dice el texto. Un C1 entiende lo que el texto *quiere decir*.

### 1.2 Estructura de respuestas de comprensión lectora

Para preguntas abiertas en examen C1, sigue este esquema:

1. **Respuesta directa** (1 frase): Contesta la pregunta
2. **Evidencia** (1-2 frases): Cita o parafrasea del texto
3. **Análisis** (1 frase, si aplica): Tu interpretación

**Ejemplo**:
> **Pregunta**: Why does the author mention Toyota?
> **Respuesta**: The author mentions Toyota to illustrate the origin of just-in-time manufacturing. *(respuesta)* Toyota pioneered this approach in the 1950s, and it became the "gold standard" for logistics. *(evidencia)* By referencing its historical success, the author emphasises how deeply ingrained this model was before recent disruptions proved its vulnerability. *(análisis)*

---

## PARTE 2: Lenguaje para Debates Formales

### 2.1 Estructura de un argumento C1

Todo argumento sólido en un debate sigue 4 pasos:

| Paso | Función | Ejemplo |
|------|---------|---------|
| **1. Claim** | Afirmar tu posición | "I firmly believe that nearshoring is the future of European logistics." |
| **2. Evidence** | Apoyar con datos | "Mexico's manufacturing sector grew 18% in 2025 due to US diversification." |
| **3. Reasoning** | Explicar POR QUÉ la evidencia apoya tu punto | "This demonstrates that companies prioritise proximity when supply chains are at risk." |
| **4. Concession + Rebuttal** | Reconocer al otro lado y contraargumentar | "While globalisation reduces unit costs, the hidden cost of disruption — $184M per incident — far outweighs these savings." |

### 2.2 Frases útiles para debatir

#### Presentar tu posición:
- "I firmly believe that..."
- "From a logistics perspective, it is clear that..."
- "The evidence overwhelmingly suggests that..."
- "What we need to understand is that..." *(cleft sentence)*

#### Dar evidencia:
- "According to recent data..."
- "A clear example of this is..."
- "If we look at the case of [country/company]..."
- "Statistics show that..."

#### Conceder y contraargumentar:
- "While I acknowledge that [opposing view]..."
- "It is true that [concession], however..."
- "Granted, [point], but what this overlooks is..."
- "My opponent may argue that..., yet..."
- "Be that as it may, the fact remains that..." *(expresión fija con subjuntivo)*

#### Cerrar con fuerza:
- "In conclusion, I would urge that we [subjuntivo]..."
- "It is imperative that companies [subjuntivo]..."
- "The question is not whether to change, but how fast."

> 📌 Fíjate cómo el lenguaje de debate se integra naturalmente con el **subjuntivo** (Clase 25) y las **cleft sentences** (Clase 23). Un C1 combina estas herramientas fluidamente.

### 2.3 Conectores para el debate

| Función | Conectores |
|---------|-----------|
| **Añadir** | furthermore, moreover, in addition, on top of that |
| **Contrastar** | however, nevertheless, conversely, on the other hand |
| **Conceder** | granted, admittedly, while it is true that |
| **Causar/Consecuencia** | consequently, therefore, as a result, thereby |
| **Ejemplificar** | for instance, a case in point, to illustrate |
| **Concluir** | ultimately, in conclusion, to sum up, all things considered |

---

## PARTE 3: Integración — Debate "Globalization vs Localization"

### Argumentos clave — Globalización (A FAVOR):

| Punto | Evidencia | Estructura C1 |
|-------|-----------|---------------|
| Costes más bajos | Manufacturing en Asia 40-60% más barato | "What globalisation offers is a 40-60% reduction in manufacturing costs." |
| Acceso a mercados | 8 mil millones de consumidores | "It is essential that companies maintain global reach to access 8 billion potential consumers." |
| Especialización | Cada país produce lo que mejor hace | "The evidence suggests that comparative advantage drives innovation." |
| Economías de escala | Volumen reduce coste unitario | "Consequently, global supply chains achieve economies of scale that local networks cannot." |

### Argumentos clave — Localización (A FAVOR):

| Punto | Evidencia | Estructura C1 |
|-------|-----------|---------------|
| Resiliencia | Menos dependencia de rutas internacionales | "It is imperative that companies reduce their dependence on fragile international routes." |
| Velocidad | Lead times de semanas → días | "What nearshoring delivers is a dramatic reduction in lead times." |
| Sostenibilidad | Menos transporte = menos CO2 | "Furthermore, local sourcing significantly reduces carbon emissions." |
| Control de calidad | Supervisión directa | "Granted, global suppliers are cheaper, but what this overlooks is the cost of quality failures." |
lessons.errors_md:
# ⚠️ ERRORES RECURRENTES — Reading Comprehension + Debate Language
**Clase 26** | Errores típicos de hispanohablantes B2→C1

---

## 1. Errores en Reading Comprehension

### 1.1 Responder con opinión cuando piden hechos
| ❌ Error | ✅ Correcto |
|----------|------------|
| "I think the author says that 73% of companies..." | "According to the text, 73% of companies experienced..." |

**Causa**: En español solemos matizar ("yo creo que dice..."). En inglés C1, si el dato está en el texto, cítalo directamente.

### 1.2 Copiar frases enteras del texto en vez de parafrasear
| ❌ Error | ✅ Correcto |
|----------|------------|
| Copiar: "The prevailing orthodoxy in logistics was clear: minimize inventory" | Parafrasear: "The dominant approach in logistics focused on keeping stock levels as low as possible." |

**Causa**: En niveles B2, copiar es un atajo. En C1, se espera parafraseo que demuestre comprensión.

### 1.3 No distinguir entre "the author says" y "the author implies"
| ❌ Error | ✅ Correcto |
|----------|------------|
| "The author says Toyota failed" (no dice eso) | "The author implies that the Toyota model has limitations in today's context." |

**Causa**: Confundir inferencia con afirmación directa. Usar "implies/suggests" para deducciones.

### 1.4 Respuestas demasiado cortas
| ❌ Error | ✅ Correcto |
|----------|------------|
| "Yes, I agree." | "I agree with the author's argument because, based on my experience at Clarel, increasing inventory by 20% during COVID prevented stockouts that cost competitors millions." |

**Causa**: Hábito del B2 de responder con una frase. C1 requiere desarrollo.

---

## 2. Errores en Debate Language

### 2.1 Empezar argumentos con "I think" en vez de estructuras formales
| ❌ Error | ✅ Correcto |
|----------|------------|
| "I think globalisation is better" | "The evidence overwhelmingly suggests that globalisation delivers superior economic outcomes." |

**Causa**: "I think" es B1-B2. C1 usa evidencia y estructuras impersonales para dar peso.

### 2.2 No conceder antes de contraargumentar
| ❌ Error | ✅ Correcto |
|----------|------------|
| "No, localisation is wrong because..." | "While I acknowledge that localisation offers greater control, the cost implications are prohibitive for most companies." |

**Causa**: En español coloquial tendemos a negar directamente. El debate formal en inglés requiere concesión primero.

### 2.3 Mezclar registros en el mismo argumento
| ❌ Error | ✅ Correcto |
|----------|------------|
| "Furthermore, I reckon we should just go for it" | "Furthermore, I firmly believe we should proceed with the implementation." |

**Causa**: Empezar formal y relajarse a medio argumento. "Reckon" y "go for it" son informales.

### 2.4 Traducir conectores literalmente del español
| Español | ❌ Calco | ✅ Correcto |
|---------|---------|------------|
| "En primer lugar" | "In first place" | "Firstly" / "First of all" |
| "Por otra parte" | "By other part" | "On the other hand" |
| "En definitiva" | "In definitive" | "Ultimately" / "In conclusion" |
| "Sin embargo" | OK ("however") | "However" / "Nevertheless" |
| "Por lo tanto" | "By the so much" ❌ | "Therefore" / "Consequently" |

### 2.5 Usar "according to me" en vez de "in my opinion"
| ❌ Error | ✅ Correcto |
|----------|------------|
| "According to me, this is the best option" | "In my opinion / From my perspective, this is the best option" |

**Causa**: Calco del español "según yo". "According to" se usa para fuentes externas, nunca para uno mismo.

---

## 3. Errores en vocabulario académico

### 3.1 Falsos amigos en registro académico
| Español | ❌ Calco | ✅ Correcto |
|---------|---------|------------|
| "Actualmente" | "Actually" (= en realidad) | "Currently" / "At present" |
| "Eventualmente" | "Eventually" (= al final) | "Possibly" / "Potentially" |
| "Argumentar" | OK ("to argue") | ✅ Pero OJO: "to argue" también = discutir |
| "Pretender" | "Pretend" (= fingir) | "Intend" / "Aim to" |
| "Soportar" | "Support" (= apoyar) | "Endure" / "Withstand" (aguantar) |
| "Realizar" | "Realise" (= darse cuenta) | "Carry out" / "Accomplish" |

### 3.2 Usar "very" en registro académico
| ❌ Error | ✅ Correcto |
|----------|------------|
| "very important" | "crucial" / "paramount" / "of utmost importance" |
| "very big" | "substantial" / "considerable" / "significant" |
| "very bad" | "detrimental" / "adverse" / "catastrophic" |

**Regla**: En texto académico/formal, sustituir "very + adjetivo" por un adjetivo más preciso.

---

## Autoevaluación rápida

¿Puedes reformular estas frases de B2 a C1?

1. "I think the text talks about supply chains." → ___
2. "No, I don't agree with globalisation." → ___
3. "In first place, costs are very important." → ___
4. "According to me, we should change." → ___
5. "The author says companies should adapt." (pero es inferencia) → ___

**Respuestas**:
1. "The text examines the shift towards supply chain resilience in an era of global disruption."
2. "While globalisation has undeniable benefits, I would contend that its risks outweigh its advantages."
3. "Firstly, cost management is of paramount importance."
4. "In my view, we should pursue this change."
5. "The author implies that companies should adapt their strategies to prioritise resilience."

lessons.content_md:
# 📖 LECCIÓN GRAMATICAL — Formal vs Informal Register en Inglés
**Clase 27** | Nivel B2→C1

---

## 1. ¿Qué es el registro lingüístico?

El **register** (registro) es el nivel de formalidad que usamos al comunicarnos. En inglés, la capacidad de cambiar de registro según el contexto es una de las marcas definitivas del nivel C1. No se trata solo de vocabulario — afecta a la gramática, los conectores, la longitud de las frases y hasta el tono.

### Los tres niveles principales:

| Registro | Cuándo se usa | Ejemplo |
|----------|--------------|---------|
| **Formal** | Emails profesionales, informes, presentaciones, contratos, academia | "I would be grateful if you could provide the documentation at your earliest convenience." |
| **Neutral** | Comunicación diaria profesional, noticias, instrucciones | "Can you send me the report by Friday?" |
| **Informal** | Amigos, familia, compañeros cercanos, WhatsApp, redes sociales | "Hey, can you get me that report? Cheers!" |

> 📌 **Diferencia con el español**: En español también cambiamos de registro (no es lo mismo un email al director que un WhatsApp a un amigo), pero los cambios en inglés son **más marcados** — el vocabulario cambia casi por completo, no solo el tono.

---

## 2. Cuándo usar cada registro

### Formal — Obligatorio en:
- Emails a superiores, clientes, personas que no conoces
- Propuestas de negocio, informes, actas
- Presentaciones a dirección o consejo
- Comunicación legal y contractual
- Artículos académicos y publicaciones
- Entrevistas de trabajo

### Neutral — Apropiado en:
- Emails entre compañeros de trabajo
- Reuniones internas del equipo
- Comunicación escrita rutinaria
- Noticias y medios generales

### Informal — Solo en:
- Mensajes a amigos y familia
- Chat interno con compañeros cercanos
- Redes sociales personales
- Conversación casual

---

## 3. Las 30 parejas clave: Informal → Formal

### Verbos

| # | Informal | Formal | Contexto de uso |
|---|----------|--------|----------------|
| 1 | **get** | **obtain / acquire** | "We need to **obtain** approval before proceeding." |
| 2 | **buy** | **purchase** | "The company will **purchase** new equipment." |
| 3 | **help** | **assist / facilitate** | "I can **assist** you with that matter." |
| 4 | **need** | **require** | "This **requires** immediate attention." |
| 5 | **ask for** | **request** | "I'd like to **request** a meeting." |
| 6 | **find out** | **determine / ascertain** | "We need to **determine** the root cause." |
| 7 | **set up** | **establish** | "They plan to **establish** a new division." |
| 8 | **deal with** | **address / handle** | "We must **address** this issue promptly." |
| 9 | **start** | **commence / initiate** | "The project will **commence** in Q2." |
| 10 | **end** | **conclude / terminate** | "The contract **terminates** in December." |
| 11 | **show** | **demonstrate / illustrate** | "The data **demonstrates** a clear trend." |
| 12 | **think about** | **consider / contemplate** | "We should **consider** the alternatives." |
| 13 | **look into** | **investigate / examine** | "HR will **investigate** the complaint." |
| 14 | **tell** | **inform / notify** | "Please **inform** the team immediately." |
| 15 | **give** | **provide / deliver** | "Can you **provide** the latest figures?" |
| 16 | **try** | **attempt / endeavour** | "We will **attempt** to resolve this today." |
| 17 | **fix** | **rectify / resolve** | "We need to **rectify** this error." |
| 18 | **keep** | **retain / maintain** | "We must **retain** top talent." |
| 19 | **use** | **utilise / employ** | "We **utilise** AI for demand forecasting." |
| 20 | **make sure** | **ensure** | "Please **ensure** compliance with regulations." |

### Adjetivos y adverbios

| # | Informal | Formal | Contexto de uso |
|---|----------|--------|----------------|
| 21 | **enough** | **sufficient / adequate** | "We have **sufficient** resources." |
| 22 | **a lot of** | **considerable / substantial** | "A **substantial** investment is required." |
| 23 | **right away** | **immediately / promptly** | "Please respond **promptly**." |
| 24 | **in the end** | **ultimately** | "**Ultimately**, the decision was unanimous." |

### Conectores

| # | Informal | Formal | Contexto de uso |
|---|----------|--------|----------------|
| 25 | **but** | **however / nevertheless** | "**However**, the results were mixed." |
| 26 | **so** | **therefore / consequently** | "**Consequently**, we revised the plan." |
| 27 | **also** | **furthermore / moreover** | "**Furthermore**, costs were reduced." |
| 28 | **about** | **approximately / regarding** | "**Regarding** your proposal, I have some concerns." |

### Expresiones

| # | Informal | Formal | Contexto de uso |
|---|----------|--------|----------------|
| 29 | **go up** | **increase / rise** | "Costs have **increased** by 12%." |
| 30 | **go down** | **decrease / decline** | "Demand has **declined** this quarter." |

---

## 4. Estructuras gramaticales formales

El registro formal no es solo vocabulario — la **gramática** también cambia:

### A) Voz pasiva (más frecuente en formal)
| Informal (activa) | Formal (pasiva) |
|-------------------|-----------------|
| "We made a mistake." | "A mistake **was made**." |
| "The team will review the report." | "The report **will be reviewed** by the team." |
| "Someone should fix this." | "This **should be rectified**." |

> 📌 La pasiva es más formal porque elimina el agente personal, haciendo el tono más impersonal y objetivo.

### B) Subjuntivo (siempre formal)
| Informal | Formal (subjuntivo) |
|----------|-------------------|
| "We should probably update the policy." | "It is recommended that the policy **be** updated." |
| "They need to come on time." | "It is imperative that they **arrive** punctually." |

### C) Construcciones impersonales
| Informal | Formal |
|----------|--------|
| "I think we should..." | "It would appear that..." / "It is advisable to..." |
| "You need to..." | "It is necessary to..." / "One should..." |
| "People say that..." | "It is widely acknowledged that..." |

### D) Preguntas indirectas (más formales que directas)
| Informal (directa) | Formal (indirecta) |
|--------------------|-------------------|
| "Where is the meeting?" | "Could you tell me where the meeting is?" |
| "When does it start?" | "I was wondering when it starts." |
| "Can I have the report?" | "I would be grateful if you could send me the report." |

---

## 5. Conectores formales vs informales

| Función | Informal | Formal |
|---------|----------|--------|
| **Añadir** | and, also, plus | furthermore, moreover, in addition, additionally |
| **Contrastar** | but, though | however, nevertheless, notwithstanding, conversely |
| **Causa** | because, so | therefore, consequently, as a result, hence |
| **Conceder** | sure, OK but... | granted, admittedly, while it is true that |
| **Ejemplificar** | like, for example | for instance, to illustrate, a case in point |
| **Concluir** | so basically... | in conclusion, ultimately, to summarise, all things considered |
| **Secuenciar** | first, then, after that | firstly, subsequently, thereafter, following this |

---

## 6. La escalera del registro en emails

Del MÁS formal al MENOS formal:

### Nivel 5 — Muy formal (contratos, legal, primera comunicación con ejecutivos)
> Dear Mr. Montalvillo,
> I am writing to formally request your attendance at the forthcoming board meeting. It would be greatly appreciated if you could confirm your availability at your earliest convenience.
> Yours faithfully,

### Nivel 4 — Formal (emails profesionales estándar)
> Dear Sergio,
> I would like to invite you to the board meeting next Tuesday. Could you please confirm whether you are available?
> Kind regards,

### Nivel 3 — Neutral (compañeros de trabajo)
> Hi Sergio,
> There's a board meeting next Tuesday. Can you let me know if you can make it?
> Thanks,

### Nivel 2 — Informal (compañeros cercanos)
> Hey Sergio,
> Board meeting Tuesday — are you in?
> Cheers,

### Nivel 1 — Muy informal (amigos, chat interno)
> Sergio — meeting Tuesday. You coming? 👍

> 📌 En el mundo profesional, lo habitual es moverse entre los niveles 3-4. El nivel 5 se reserva para situaciones muy específicas. El error más común de hispanohablantes es quedarse siempre en nivel 2-3 incluso cuando la situación exige nivel 4-5.

---

## 7. Registro académico y de negocios

### Características del registro académico:
- **Evitar contracciones**: "do not" en vez de "don't"
- **Evitar "very"**: usar adjetivos más precisos ("significant" en vez de "very big")
- **Evitar primera persona**: "It can be argued that..." en vez de "I think that..."
- **Nominalización**: usar sustantivos en vez de verbos ("the implementation of" en vez de "when we implement")
- **Hedging** (matizar): "This appears to suggest..." en vez de "This proves..."

### Características del registro de negocios:
- **Acción clara**: "I recommend that we proceed" (no vaguedades)
- **Datos cuantificados**: "a 15% reduction" (no "a big drop")
- **Cortesía formulada**: "I would appreciate..." / "At your convenience..."
- **Call to action**: siempre cerrar con lo que esperas del lector

---

## 8. Comparación con el español

| Aspecto | Español | Inglés |
|---------|---------|--------|
| **Grado de cambio** | Moderado (el vocabulario cambia menos) | Radical (el vocabulario cambia casi por completo) |
| **Tú/Usted** | Marca clara de formalidad | No existe — la formalidad se marca con vocabulario y estructuras |
| **Contracciones** | No son un marcador de registro | Son un marcador clave (formal = sin contracciones) |
| **Pasiva** | Se usa menos que en inglés formal | Marca de formalidad muy fuerte |
| **Conectores** | Menos diferencia entre formal e informal | Cambio radical ("but" → "however/nevertheless") |

> 📌 En español, puedes sonar formal solo con cambiar de "tú" a "usted". En inglés, necesitas cambiar vocabulario + gramática + conectores + tono. Es un cambio más complejo.

---

## 9. Cheat Sheet — Registro Formal

```
✅ HAGO EN FORMAL:
- Vocabulario específico (obtain, require, establish, demonstrate)
- Conectores académicos (furthermore, consequently, notwithstanding)
- Voz pasiva cuando apropiado
- Frases completas y bien estructuradas
- Cortesía formulada ("I would be grateful if...")
- Sin contracciones (do not, I am, it is)

❌ EVITO EN FORMAL:
- Phrasal verbs (get, put up with, look into → acquire, tolerate, investigate)
- Contracciones (don't, can't, I'm)
- Slang / coloquialismos (gonna, wanna, stuff, cool)
- "Very" + adjetivo básico
- Primera persona excesiva
- Emojis / signos de exclamación múltiples
```
lessons.errors_md:
# ⚠️ ERRORES RECURRENTES — Formal vs Informal Register
**Clase 27** | Errores típicos de hispanohablantes B2→C1

---

## 1. Mezcla de registros (el error más común)

Empezar un email formal y "relajarse" a la mitad. En inglés, mezclar registros suena **jarring** (chirriante) — mucho más que en español.

| ❌ Error (mezcla) | ✅ Correcto (consistente) |
|-------------------|-------------------------|
| "I am writing to inform you that we've gotta sort out the delivery problem." | "I am writing to inform you that we need to rectify the delivery issue as a matter of urgency." |
| "Furthermore, I reckon we should just go for it." | "Furthermore, I firmly believe we should proceed with the implementation." |
| "The aforementioned issues are kinda important." | "The aforementioned issues require immediate attention." |

**Regla**: Elige tu registro al principio y **mantente en él** todo el texto. Si no estás seguro, siempre es mejor pasarse de formal que quedarse corto.

---

## 2. Usar phrasal verbs en registro formal

Los **phrasal verbs** (get, put, look, take + partícula) son informales por naturaleza. En contexto formal, sustituye SIEMPRE por el verbo de origen latino/francés:

| ❌ Informal (phrasal verb) | ✅ Formal (verbo latino) |
|---------------------------|------------------------|
| "We need to **find out** what happened." | "We need to **determine/ascertain** what happened." |
| "Can you **look into** this?" | "Could you **investigate/examine** this?" |
| "We have to **set up** a meeting." | "We need to **arrange/establish** a meeting." |
| "I want to **bring up** an issue." | "I wish to **raise** an issue." |
| "We should **put off** the decision." | "We should **postpone/defer** the decision." |
| "They **came up with** a solution." | "They **devised/proposed** a solution." |
| "We need to **get rid of** old stock." | "We need to **dispose of/eliminate** old stock." |

> 📌 **¿Por qué pasa esto?** Los hispanohablantes aprenden phrasal verbs en B1-B2 y los usan orgullosamente en B2. Pero en C1, hay que saber cuándo NO usarlos. Es como usar "mola" en una carta al director — técnicamente correcto, contextualmente inapropiado.

---

## 3. Exceso de formalidad (over-formality)

El error opuesto: sonar tan formal que resulta artificial o anticuado. Suele pasar por calcar el español formal demasiado literalmente.

| ❌ Demasiado formal | ✅ Nivel correcto |
|--------------------|--------------------|
| "I humbly beseech you to consider our proposal." | "I would appreciate your consideration of our proposal." |
| "It would be my greatest honour to..." | "I would welcome the opportunity to..." |
| "I remain your most obedient servant." | "Kind regards," |
| "Herewith enclosed please find the documentation." | "Please find attached the documentation." |

**Regla**: El inglés formal moderno es **conciso y directo**, no florido. "I would appreciate..." es el nivel correcto para negocios. No necesitas sonar como una carta del siglo XVIII.

---

## 4. Falsos amigos de registro

Palabras que en español son formales pero en inglés significan otra cosa, o viceversa:

| Español formal | ❌ Calco incorrecto | ✅ Correcto en inglés |
|---------------|--------------------|-----------------------|
| "Actualmente" | "Actually" (= en realidad) | "Currently" / "At present" |
| "Eventualmente" | "Eventually" (= al final) | "Possibly" / "Potentially" |
| "Realizar una reunión" | "Realise a meeting" (= darse cuenta) | "Hold/conduct a meeting" |
| "Asistir a una reunión" | "Assist a meeting" (= ayudar) | "Attend a meeting" |
| "Resumir" | "Resume" (= retomar) | "Summarise" |
| "Pretender" | "Pretend" (= fingir) | "Intend" / "Aim to" |
| "Constatar" | No existe en inglés estándar | "Verify" / "Confirm" |
| "Concretar" | "Concrete" (= hormigón/concreto) | "Specify" / "Define" / "Finalise" |

---

## 5. No adaptar las contracciones al registro

En español no hay contracciones opcionales (excepto "al" y "del"), así que los hispanohablantes no registran la diferencia. En inglés, las contracciones son un **marcador de registro crítico**:

| Contexto | ❌ Error | ✅ Correcto |
|----------|---------|------------|
| Email al CEO | "We can't deliver on time." | "We are unable to deliver on time." |
| Informe anual | "It's been a good year." | "It has been a positive year." |
| Propuesta formal | "We don't have enough budget." | "We do not have sufficient budget." |
| Chat con compañero | "I do not believe that is correct." | "I don't think that's right." (¡aquí SÍ!) |

**Regla**: 
- **Formal/Académico** → NUNCA contracciones
- **Neutral** → Contracciones opcionales
- **Informal** → Contracciones siempre (no usarlas suena robótico)

---

## 6. Usar "Dear Sir/Madam" cuando sabes el nombre

| ❌ Error | ✅ Correcto |
|---------|------------|
| "Dear Sir/Madam," (cuando sabes que es María García) | "Dear Ms García," / "Dear María," |
| "To whom it may concern," (en un email directo) | "Dear [nombre]," |

**Regla**: "Dear Sir/Madam" y "To whom it may concern" solo cuando REALMENTE no sabes quién va a leer el email. Si sabes el nombre, úsalo.

---

## 7. Cerrar emails con el nivel equivocado

| Nivel | ❌ Error de hispanohablantes | ✅ Correcto |
|-------|------------------------------|------------|
| Muy formal | "Kisses" / "Hugs" (traducción literal) | "Yours faithfully," / "Yours sincerely," |
| Formal | "A greeting" (calco de "Un saludo") | "Kind regards," / "Best regards," |
| Neutral | "Regards," (a un amigo) | "Best," / "Cheers," / "Thanks," |
| Informal | "Kind regards," (a tu mejor amigo) | "Later!" / "Talk soon!" / solo el nombre |

---

## 8. Nominalización insuficiente

En inglés formal, se prefieren sustantivos sobre verbos (nominalización). Los hispanohablantes tienden a usar verbos incluso en contexto formal:

| ❌ Verbal (menos formal) | ✅ Nominal (más formal) |
|-------------------------|------------------------|
| "We need to implement the system." | "The **implementation** of the system is required." |
| "When we analyse the data..." | "Upon **analysis** of the data..." |
| "We failed to comply." | "There was a **failure** of **compliance**." |
| "We decided to invest." | "The **decision** to invest was made." |

---

## Autoevaluación rápida

Reformula a registro formal:

1. "Hey, we gotta fix this ASAP." → ___
2. "I think we should probably look into it." → ___
3. "The meeting's at 3. Don't be late." → ___
4. "We've got a lot of problems right now." → ___
5. "Can you find out who did it?" → ___

**Respuestas**:
1. "I am writing to inform you that this matter requires immediate rectification."
2. "I would recommend that we investigate this matter further."
3. "The meeting is scheduled for 3 PM. Punctual attendance is appreciated."
4. "We are currently facing a considerable number of challenges."
5. "Could you determine/ascertain who was responsible?"
